claim: Codex is a software engineering agent that can run commands, tests, explore codebases, and perform all tasks a human software engineer would do
actionable: Use Codex not just for code generation but for test execution, repository exploration, and full end-to-end feature implementation workflows.

claim: Websockets provide 1.75x faster token serving speed and fast mode adds an additional 2x speed improvement when using Codex
actionable: Enable websocket connections and fast mode when using Codex for real-time responsiveness, especially for interactive code generation tasks.

claim: Plugins bundle skills, apps (service integrations), and MCP servers together to avoid manual setup of multiple individual connections
actionable: Create plugins that combine related skills (Playwright for browser testing, Imagen for asset generation) and integrations so users get a complete package.

claim: Automations in Codex can run on scheduled intervals (cron jobs) while integrating apps like Slack and Gmail to filter and summarize information
actionable: Set up automations that check email/Slack at specific times, filter for time-sensitive messages, and bucket information by topic before sending summaries.

claim: Codex code review finds second-order effects and changes in unmodified modules that simple diff-based code review tools miss
actionable: Run Codex code review on pull requests to detect unintended side effects and impacts on related modules before merging.

claim: 100% of pull requests at OpenAI are reviewed by Codex code review as the first pass before human review
actionable: Integrate Codex code review as a mandatory CI step in your GitHub workflows to catch issues before human reviewers see them.

claim: Sub-agents can be spun off from a main task to explore different approaches in parallel, then collate results for synthesis
actionable: For complex tasks, ask Codex to create multiple sub-agents with different hypotheses to approach the same problem, then merge their findings.

claim: Sub-agents can be configured with different models, reasoning effort, sandbox modes, and MCP/skill permissions for specialized tasks
actionable: Define review agents in read-only mode, execution agents with write access, and give each access only to the tools they need (e.g., Sentry for one, Linear for another).

claim: Guardian Approvals is an experimental feature that spins up a separate sub-agent to verify whether privileged operations need human approval
actionable: Enable guardian approvals in experimental settings to reduce human fatigue from constant approval prompts while maintaining safety gates.

claim: Hooks allow programmatic actions on Codex events: startup (pull latest code), after tool use (log actions), and stop (keep going for long tasks)
actionable: Define hooks in hooks.json to auto-pull from GitHub on startup and run a keep_going.py script on stop events for continuous long-running tasks.

claim: Codex crossed 3 million weekly active users as of the recent milestone with adoption tripling since January
actionable: Monitor community adoption trends to understand what use cases are driving Codex growth and align your own usage accordingly.

claim: GPT-5.3 and GPT-5.4 are the leading models for Codex, with Spark being a fast variant optimized for speed on Cerebras
actionable: Use GPT-5.4 for complex reasoning tasks and Spark for quick iterations where latency matters more than depth.

claim: Work trees in Codex allow parallel work on multiple features within the same project without context switching or interfering with tasks
actionable: Use native work tree support in Codex to maintain separate feature branches and switch between them without full context reload.
